Statement0/0
    ← Vibes

    ★ new + delightful

    Say hello with a bounce.

    Rounded corners, spring-loaded motion, and a confetti cannon on every tap. Serotonin, shipped as a design system.

    or just say hi →
    Springy
    Squishy
    Confetti
    ★ WELCOME TO MY HOMEPAGE ★ SIGN MY GUESTBOOK ★ 56K WARNING ★ ★ WELCOME TO MY HOMEPAGE ★ SIGN MY GUESTBOOK ★ 56K WARNING ★ 

    made with love + way too much serotonin

    visitors 086000

    Same markup, same layout -- only the stylesheet changed. Hit the skins up top ↑.

    ❯ The skin-swapEssential

    Most 'vibes' aren't actually different machines -- they're one layout wearing a different stylesheet. Swap a set of theme tokens, and this exact markup turns from a toy into a terminal into a spaceship. That's not really a trick; it's basically how a design system is supposed to be built.

    How it's done

    One HTML skeleton. Each skin is a block of CSS custom properties (color, radius, font, shadow, border-width) on a [data-skin] attribute -- the toggle just changes the attribute. Zero layout code is duplicated.

    ★ wobble on hover

    Delight, on tap.

    Boing
    Squish
    Pop

    Playful

    Spring easing, morphing blobs, wobble-on-hover, confetti. Probably the friendliest skin in the set -- and the easiest one to overdo.

    ❯ Confetti & springVanity

    Confetti is basically a dopamine hit -- delightful the first time, noise every time after that. Springy motion and wobble sell a warm brand, but the celebration should stay rare -- save it for a real win (a finished signup), not every single click.

    How it's done

    Spring feel is an easing curve; the burst spawns short-lived DOM particles animated with GSAP, and it's skipped entirely under prefers-reduced-motion.

    HELLO WORLD

    RAW HTML ENERGY

    Unstyled, on purpose. Here is a real 1995 link.

    Brutalist

    System mono, 3px borders, zero radius, snap hovers, and links that look like they did back in Netscape. It refuses to be pretty on purpose.

    ❯ Brutalist raw HTMLSituational

    Hard borders, mono type, and 1995-blue links read as honest and memorable -- perfect for a gallery, a zine, or a dev tool with some attitude. On a mainstream storefront though, the same look just reads as broken, and pushes normal users away. Deploy it on purpose, for an audience that actually gets the joke.

    How it's done

    Pure CSS: 3px solid borders, hard offset box-shadows with no blur, and transition: none for a deliberately un-animated, snappy hover.

    ◆ 0x…c0ffee

    CONNECT.

    Web3 / Futuristic

    Dark canvas, aurora haze, a Tron floor, drifting particles, glitching type, and a button that really wants your wallet.

    ❯ Aurora, Tron grid & glowVanity

    Neon glow signals 'the future' -- but glow isn't trust. This look launders credibility for projects that often haven't actually earned it, and users have learned to read heavy glow as a yellow flag. Gorgeous, and increasingly kind of a liability.

    How it's done

    The grid is a perspective-transformed repeating-gradient scrolling toward you; the glow is layered box- and text-shadow; particles are a few CSS-animated dots; the glitch is two clipped pseudo-copies of the text. All motion is gated behind reduced-motion.

    ✧ best viewed in 800×600

    WELCOME!

    ★ SIGN MY GUESTBOOK ★ 56K WARNING ★ UNDER CONSTRUCTION ★ ★ SIGN MY GUESTBOOK ★ 56K WARNING ★ UNDER CONSTRUCTION ★ 

    Visitors: 011000

    Retro / Y2K

    Chrome gradients, CRT scanlines, a marquee that will not quit, and a visitor counter that's definitely real. Nostalgia, bottled.

    ❯ Chrome, CRT & counterVanity

    Chrome text, scanlines, a marquee, and a hit counter are pure nostalgia -- a great limited-run gag, or a music/art microsite. As a lasting brand though, it dates in months and fights readability (scanlines over body text are basically eye strain). Fun, with a famously short shelf life.

    How it's done

    Chrome type is a metallic gradient clipped to the glyphs; scanlines are a repeating-linear-gradient overlay; the counter ticks up once when it scrolls into view -- honestly, from where the markup starts.

    Fake depth, real hover.

    Move your cursor across this card. It leans toward you like it's floating a few millimetres off the page -- but there's no depth there really, just a perspective transform reading your mouse and tilting to match. Convincing, and completely cosmetic.

    tilt me
    ❯ 3D tiltVanity

    The card that leans toward your cursor sells 'premium' and 'interactive' in one gesture -- and it's basically a parlour trick, easy to overdo into seasickness. One tasteful hero element is charming. A whole grid tilting at once is a headache. Depth's the costume here, not the feature.

    How it's done

    pointermove maps the cursor's position inside the card to a small rotateX / rotateY under CSS perspective, recentred on leave. Skipped on touch and under prefers-reduced-motion.

    There's a code hidden in here.

    The oldest easter egg on the web:↑↑↓↓←→←→BA. Type it now -- or just tap the pad below. Something ridiculous is about to happen, and absolutely nothing about the product's going to improve.

    ❯ Konami codeVanity

    A secret handshake for the people who already know to look for it. It builds zero trust, converts no one, and delights nobody who isn't already delighted to be here -- which is kind of the point. Pure joy, pure vanity, and worth it precisely because it costs so little to hide.

    How it's done

    A keydown listener matches the last ten keys against the sequence; a hit fires the same confetti burst as the playful skin. The on-screen pad feeds the same matcher so touch users get in too.

    That's the Statement.

    Same skeleton -- the vibe is just a stylesheet.
